abstract: It is widely recognized that the biological interactions of nanoparticles (NPs) are dictated by the "protein corona"—the layer of serum proteins that spontaneously adsorbs onto the NP surface. While it is established that the corona’s composition depends on the biological media, we hypothesized that the unique physiological changes associated with specific diseases would result in distinct, disease-specific coronas. To investigate this, we incubated polystyrene and silica nanoparticles with plasma samples obtained from donors across a broad spectrum of medical conditions, including breast cancer, diabetes, rheumatism, hypercholesterolemia, and pregnancy, among others. Our findings reveal that the specific underlying disease or physiological state significantly alters the protein fingerprint of the corona. Consequently, we introduce the concept of the "personalized protein corona" (PPC) as a critical factor in nanomedicine. These results suggest that future diagnostic and therapeutic nanocarriers must be tailored to the PPC/individual patient profile to ensure clinical efficacy and safety.
